itineraries.title: Turkmenistan Circuit: Ashgabat, Karakum Desert and the Caspian Sea
itineraries.subtitle: From the marble capital to the flames of Darvaza, from medieval Silk Road cities to the Caspian coast

visits.text: The National Museum of Turkmenistan occupies an eight-pointed star building at the far end of Independence Park. Its collections span 6,000 years of history, from the Jeitun civilization to the ivory rhytons of Nisa. Allow 90 minutes and expect an English-speaking guide—they're mandatory and included in the ticket price.

Eighteen kilometers west of the city center, the Parthian ruins of Nisa plunge you into the history of the 3rd century BC. As the first capital of the Parthian Empire, the site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2007. Two distinct fortresses stand here: Old Nisa (residential) and New Nisa (dynastic).

The Independence Monument soars to 118 meters, crowned with a golden crescent. The 140-hectare park surrounding it is lined with fountains, statues of Turkmen heroes, and immaculate lawns. Stroll here at sunset: the 4.5 million square meters of white marble reflect an otherworldly light.

activities.text: When evening falls, Ashgabat's avenues light up and empty out. Only white cars cruise the streets—an unofficial rule but one that's strictly observed. Walk along Garashsyzlyk Avenue to grasp the strangeness of this city-as-stage-set: $14 billion spent on a film set with no actors.

map_keywords: Alem Ferris Wheel cultural center Ashgabat

visits.text: The Turkmenbashi Ruhy Mosque in Gypjak, 4 km from the center, is the country's largest: four 91-meter minarets, a 50-meter main dome, and capacity for 10,000 worshippers. It adjoins the mausoleum of Niyazov, Turkmenistan's first president, whose name is inscribed in golden letters.

The Carpet Museum houses nearly 2,000 pieces, including a 301 m² specimen that entered the Guinness Book of Records in 2001—the world's largest hand-knotted carpet. The gül patterns specific to each Turkmen tribe function like a visual alphabet that staff members are delighted to decode for you.

The Alem Center houses what was the world's largest enclosed Ferris wheel when it opened in 2012: 57 meters in diameter, built by Italy's Fabbri Group for $90 million. Yet another record in a city that collects them.

activities.text: From the cable car station at the foot of the Kopet Dag, the ascent to 1,290 meters offers a striking panorama of Ashgabat's white marble expanse. On clear days, Iranian ridges close the horizon to the south. Allow 90 minutes round-trip.

map_keywords: Kow Ata bat colony cave Bäherden

visits.text: One hundred kilometers west of Ashgabat, Bakharden Cave conceals a 70-meter-long thermal lake 55 meters below ground. The water maintains a constant 36°C and contains 38 mineral elements including sulfur, iodine, and magnesium—hence its distinctive smell. The descent requires 250 to 350 lit steps; changing cabins await below. Home to the largest bat colony in the former USSR.

activities.text: After your swim, the road continues north through the first ridges of the Karakum. The landscape shifts rapidly: the foothills of the Kopet Dag give way to the vast, ochre flatness of the desert. This is where the true Turkmen adventure begins.

to_next_leg.road_type: M37 heading north then 4x4 desert track

visits.text: Yerbent is a hamlet of herders halfway between Kow Ata and Darvaza. The stop lasts an hour—just long enough to step inside a yurt and accept the fried bread and milk tea that the local teahouse offers the rare visitors passing through.

activities.text: Explore the yurts and meet the local camel population

map_keywords: Darvaza gas crater Gates of Hell Turkmenistan

visits.text: The Darvaza crater—officially the "Reflection of Karakum"—measures 60 to 70 meters in diameter and 30 meters deep. In 1971, Soviet drilling pierced a methane-filled cavern whose roof collapsed; engineers lit the gas to prevent poisoning nearby villagers. In 2025, authorities reduced the flames by two-thirds by drilling capture wells around the crater—but the nocturnal spectacle remains intact.

A few kilometers from the fire crater, two other craters merit a detour: one filled with greenish water where gas bubbles rise to the surface, the other with bubbling gray mud. Together, the three form a planetary landscape with no equivalent anywhere.

activities.text: The night in a yurt camp at the crater's edge is the circuit's highlight. Dinner by barbecue in the absolute silence of the Karakum, then return to the rim after 9 PM: even reduced, the fire cuts through the desert darkness with an orange glow unlike anything else.

to_next_leg.road_type: Desert track then M37 north to Konye-Urgench

visits.text: The Kutlug Timur Minaret dominates the UNESCO site of Konye-Urgench: 60 meters high, with a base 12 meters in diameter tapering to 2 meters at the summit, featuring 18 decorative bands in fired brick, three bearing Kufic inscriptions. It's the tallest medieval monument in the region after Afghanistan's Minaret of Jam. Construction began in the 11th century, completed between 1320 and 1330 under Kutlug Timur. It leans slightly at the top—likely earthquake damage.

The Turabek Khanum Mausoleum, built for Kutlug Timur's wife, displays a dome covered with star-and-flower mosaics—a 14th-century technical masterpiece whose influence reached as far as Mughal architecture in India. The Il Arslan Mausoleum (12th c.) is the oldest standing monument on the site.

The Sultan Tekesh Mausoleum (12th c.) is recognizable by its 30-meter blue conical dome, visible like a beacon across the plain. Here, in 1333, Ibn Battuta described Konye-Urgench as "the greatest and most beautiful of Turkic cities."

activities.text: Walking through this UNESCO site (listed 2005) in late afternoon, when the raking light gilds the fired brick, completely transforms how you read the monuments. Konye-Urgench was destroyed by Genghis Khan in 1221 then razed by Tamerlane in 1388—what remains is all the more precious.

visits.text: Dashoguz is the capital of northern Turkmenistan, 50 km from Konye-Urgench. The city has no major sights, but its central bazaar offers a glimpse of provincial Turkmen life far from Ashgabat's marble avenues—a useful contrast before catching your flight to Mary.

to_next_leg.road_type: Domestic flight Dashoguz (TAZ) → Mary (MYP), direct, ~50 min. ⚠️ Only 2 flights/week—confirm connection with your agency

map_keywords: Ancient Merv UNESCO State Park Mary Turkmenistan

visits.text: Start with Mary's History Museum to contextualize the collections from Merv and Gonur Depe—background knowledge radically changes how you read the ruins. Then head 30 km east to ancient Merv: 353 hectares of archaeological park listed as UNESCO in 1999, with 4,000 years of continuous history.

The Sultan Sanjar Mausoleum (1157) dominates ancient Merv at 27 meters. During his reign, the city had an estimated population of 500,000—the world's most populous city in the 12th century. The walls are 5 meters thick. Its original turquoise dome disappeared during the Mongol sack of 1221; what you see today is a 20th-century restoration. Nearby: Erk Kala and Gyaur Kala fortresses, Shahriyar Ark citadel, Askhab mausoleums (Islamic pilgrimage site).

Gonur Depe, 90 km north of Mary, was the capital of the Margiana civilization (2300-1500 BC). Discovered by Soviet archaeologist Viktor Sarianidi in 1972, the site reveals a royal palace, necropolis, and Zoroastrian fire altars—one of Central Asia's oldest civilizations. Accessible only by 4x4 on a track, 2 hours each way.

activities.text: Explore this Silk Road oasis and 3rd-century BC settlement

to_next_leg.road_type: Flight Mary (MYP) → Turkmenbashi (KRW) via Ashgabat, ~1h15 total

visits.text: Awaza is the anti-Karakum: 5,000 hectares of seaside resort built from 2007 onward on the eastern shore of the Caspian Sea, 12 km from Turkmenbashi. Eighteen hotels, a 7-km artificial canal, a water park opened in 2018, and a fountain complex with 24-meter jets synchronized to music. Nicknamed "Turkmen Las Vegas," the zone receives roughly 150,000 visitors per year—almost exclusively Turkmen. You'll be among the rare foreigners.

activities.text: Swimming in the Caspian or a stroll along Awaza's canal—the contrast with yesterday's sand tracks is striking. Evening return flight Turkmenbashi (KRW) → Ashgabat (ASB), 2 to 6 daily flights depending on season.

visits.text: Independence Square
Ruhnama Monument
National Museum of Turkmenistan
Turkmenbashi Ruhy Mosque in Gypjak
Ashgabat cable car to Kopet Dag mountains

activities.text: Return to Ashgabat after the Turkmenbashi flight. Free morning for last-minute shopping—miniature carpets, silver belts, telpek hats made of astrakhan—before your international flight. Ashgabat (ASB) is the country's only exit point.

seo.h1_title: Turkmenistan Circuit: Ashgabat, Darvaza Crater and the Caspian Sea
seo.intro_paragraph: Ashgabat collects Guinness World Records the way other cities collect inhabitants: 543 white marble buildings across 22 km², the world's largest enclosed Ferris wheel, the world's largest hand-knotted carpet. To the north, the Karakum stretches 350,000 km² to the Darvaza crater—60 to 70 meters in diameter, ignited in 1971, still burning. The circuit climbs to Konye-Urgench, where Ibn Battuta saw "the most beautiful of Turkic cities," crosses ancient Merv whose population exceeded 500,000 in the 12th century, then ends on the Caspian coast at Awaza. Turkmenistan resembles nothing else.
seo.seo_hook: A crater lit in 1971 that still burns today. A capital where only white cars are allowed. Eleventh-century minarets in the middle of the desert. The Caspian Sea at the end of 3,000 km of track. This is what Turkmenistan has in store for those who dare to go.
featured_list: Darvaza: methane crater ignited 1971, 60-70 m diameter, still active
Ashgabat: 543 white marble buildings, Guinness record since 2013
Merv: UNESCO oasis, ~500,000 inhabitants in 12th c., world's largest city
Awaza: pharaonic resort on Caspian Sea, 18 hotels, 7-km canal

places_taglines.TM::ashgabat: Where white marble meets world records—543 buildings across twenty-two square kilometers
places_taglines.TM::kowata: A subterranean lake where thermal waters and the USSR's largest bat colony hide fifty-five meters below ground
places_taglines.TM::yerbent: Where desert herders still brew milk tea for the handful of travelers crossing the Karakum
places_taglines.TM::darvaza: A seventy-meter crater that's been burning since Soviet engineers lit it half a century ago
places_taglines.TM::konyeurgench: Where a sixty-meter medieval minaret rises from ruins that Genghis Khan and Tamerlane both destroyed
places_taglines.TM::mary: The gateway to Merv—once home to half a million souls and the world's greatest city
places_taglines.TM::turkmenbashi: Where marble hotels and synchronized fountains rise from the eastern shore of the Caspian
places_taglines.TM::dashoguz: A provincial capital whose bazaar offers respite between ancient monuments and modern airports

highlights: In 2025, the Darvaza flames were partially reduced by capture wells drilled around the crater—the site remains active but the fire is less intense than before 2022. Check current status with your agency before departure.
All trips require an official invitation (LOI) obtained through a local agency before visa application—allow 2 to 4 weeks lead time.
Transport only by 4x4 with official guide for desert stages (Karakum, Gonur Depe); paved roads link Ashgabat, Mary, and Turkmenbashi.

Ashgabat (ASB) is the only international airport; domestic flights: ASB↔MYP (Mary), ASB↔KRW (Turkmenbashi), ASB↔TAZ (Dashoguz). The TAZ→MYP flight is direct (50 min) but operates only twice weekly—verify with your agency to align dates.
Merv (UNESCO 1999) deserves at minimum a half-day; Gonur Depe requires a full day from Mary (90 km track, 4x4 essential).
